title: How I Learned To Love the Bubble (Even Before it Bursts) - Hussman Funds
gist: Hussman argues the S&P 500 sits at record valuation extremes (MarketCap/GVA, forward P/E) marking a third great speculative bubble beyond 1929 and 2000, requiring a 70%+ retreat to reach historical norms, but says an enhanced September 2024 hedging framework now lets his funds profit from bubble advances via a safety net without needing the bubble to burst.

insights.claim: MarketCap/GVA (the ratio of nonfinancial market capitalization to gross value-added, an economy-wide price/revenue multiple) sits at its most extreme level since data begin in 1928, and Hussman calls it the valuation gauge best correlated with actual subsequent market returns across a century of market cycles.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: A revenue-based valuation metric strips out the effect of elevated profit margins, so investors relying on P/E alone may be understating true valuation extremity.
insights.locator: Record extremes

insights.claim: Even taking Wall Street analysts' optimistic forward earnings estimates at face value and assuming record corporate profit margins are permanent, the S&P 500 price-to-forward-operating-earnings ratio still rivals the most extreme levels in history.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: The bull case that elevated margins justify current multiples fails even on its own generous assumptions, removing a common valuation defense.
insights.locator: Record extremes

insights.claim: Record U.S. corporate profit margins are the precise accounting-identity mirror image of record deficits elsewhere in the economy: households (especially the bottom 90%, who collectively own less than 13% of U.S. equities) and government, where two-thirds of spending is transfer payments bridging the gap left by wages now at a record-low share of GDP.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Elevated margins are structurally tied to fiscal deficits and depressed labor share, not sustainable efficiency gains — a fiscal tightening or wage-share normalization would mechanically compress margins and expose true valuation levels.
insights.quote: primarily households (especially the 90% that collectively own less than 13% of U.S. equities)
insights.locator: These record corporate profits are the precise mirror image

insights.claim: At current valuation extremes, the market would need to fall by more than 70% just to reach historical valuation norms.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Quantifies the tail risk Hussman is hedging against; useful as a stress-test benchmark for portfolio downside scenarios.
insights.quote: would require a market retreat of more than 70% simply to reach historical norms
insights.locator: Our present outlook continues to fluctuate mostly on the defensive side

insights.claim: Hussman characterizes the current market as the third great speculative bubble in U.S. financial history, beyond both 1929 and 2000.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Frames the current episode as historically rare and extreme, implying analogues should be drawn from 1929 and 2000 rather than typical cyclical corrections.
insights.quote: the third great speculative bubble in U.S. history – beyond both 1929 and 2000
insights.locator: Yet even as I discuss what I view as the third great speculative bubble

insights.claim: Hussman's core statistical framework is that unconditional historical return distributions (e.g., ~10% average annual S&P 500 return) are misleading once conditioned on valuation and market-internals data; the resulting conditional distributions still contain both positive and negative outcomes but with very different averages and volatility.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Investors should treat valuation/internals-conditioned return distributions, not long-run historical averages, as the relevant base rate for current positioning.
insights.locator: Whatever market conditions may be, it can help to look at the probability distribution

insights.claim: Using a crude classification (Shiller CAPE-implied yield versus 10-year bonds, plus the percentage of stocks above their 200-day average), the unfavorable conditional distribution of weekly S&P 500 returns since 1940 lags T-bills by about 2% annualized and is the widest (most volatile), while the favorable conditional distribution beats T-bills by close to 12% annualized and is the narrowest (least volatile).
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Unfavorable market regimes aren't just lower-return, they're also higher-volatility, meaning risk-adjusted underperformance during 'bearish' conditions is worse than a simple average-return comparison suggests.
insights.locator: The blue curve is the unconditional probability distribution

insights.claim: Using Hussman's actual proprietary market classification (not just crude valuation/internals proxies), periods classified bearish have lagged T-bills by about 22% annualized while periods classified leverage/constructive have beaten T-bills by about 28% annualized, a pattern consistent across history from the current bubble back to the Great Depression.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: The magnitude of the spread (50 points of annualized relative performance) is the quantitative justification for Hussman's regime-based hedging rather than static allocation.
insights.locator: While the overall profile of these distributions is similar

insights.claim: The popular warning about 'missing the 10 best days/weeks' of market returns is misleading because those outsized rewarding periods generally occur during market crashes, not calm bull markets.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Undercuts the common buy-and-hold argument for staying fully invested at all times; the 'best days' are clustered in exactly the volatile, hedged periods Hussman's discipline is designed to navigate rather than avoid.
insights.quote: these “rewarding” instances generally occur during periods when the market is crashing
insights.locator: When people say how much investors would lose by missing the 10 best

insights.claim: Hussman views 10-year Treasury yields as only marginally adequate, providing no material risk-adjusted edge over T-bills, translating to an overall 'marginally constructive' bond stance.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Bonds are not currently a strong relative-value opportunity in Hussman's framework, just a mild positive tilt rather than a high-conviction allocation.
insights.locator: In the bond market, we presently view 10-year Treasury yields

insights.claim: Historically, essentially the entire excess total return of Treasury bonds over T-bill returns has accrued only during periods when yields were above two simple observable benchmarks Hussman tracks.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: A simple, replicable yield-level screen has historically captured essentially all of bonds' excess return, making it a useful gauge for timing duration exposure.
insights.locator: The chart below shows the 10-year yield relative to simple, observable benchmarks

insights.claim: The entire historical total return of the Philadelphia Gold & Silver Index (XAU) has accrued during periods of falling 10-year Treasury yields; given gold's runup over the past year, a firming NAPM purchasing managers index, and the absence of upward pressure on jobless claims, Hussman's gold/silver outlook is less constructive than during most of 2025, though still 'modestly constructive.'
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Gold/silver miners' further upside is conditioned on continued yield declines; a firming PMI and stable labor market are early signals that the tailwind behind the recent precious-metals runup may be fading.
insights.locator: Similarly, in the precious metals market

insights.claim: Hussman rebuts an unnamed article's claim that nearly all investment mathematics is 'almost 100% phony,' arguing that the phrase 'almost 100%' is itself a better example of sham mathematics than anything it criticizes.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Signals Hussman's broader defense of quantitative, valuation-based investing against a growing 'math is useless in markets' narrative.
insights.quote: nearly all mathematics in the field of investment is “sham” and “almost 100% phony.”
insights.locator: Mathematics as a tool, versus mathematics as a dogma

insights.claim: Mathematics itself is not the problem in finance; it is merely structured, systematic thinking, and it only becomes a 'sham' when paired with dogmatic assumptions — such as treating stock prices as a pure random walk, assuming perfect lognormal bell curves without skew or fat tails, or assuming cross-asset correlations stay well-behaved during a crisis.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Investors should scrutinize the assumptions embedded in a model (correlation stability, distribution shape) rather than rejecting quantitative methods wholesale.
insights.locator: Now, yes, if one’s use of mathematics in finance assumes

insights.claim: Leveraging a portfolio 40-to-1 on the assumption that cross-asset correlations would remain well-behaved is what caused the collapse of Long-Term Capital Management; slicing up mortgage risk into securities assumed to behave well in a crisis is what blew up Lehman and other banks during the global financial crisis.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Both historical blowups trace to the same root cause (assumed correlation stability under stress), a pattern worth checking for in any current strategy relying on diversification or structured products.
insights.quote: leading you to lever your portfolio up 40-to-1 (which is what blew up Long-Term Capital Management)
insights.locator: leading you to lever your portfolio up 40-to-1

insights.claim: Option pricing does not require assuming a lognormal ('bell curve') distribution of returns; any probability distribution can be used as long as the probabilities sum to 1 and the resulting expected value rules out arbitrage.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Options-based hedging strategies (like Hussman's own) need not rely on the same fragile distributional assumptions that broke down for LTCM or mortgage-securitization models.
insights.locator: You can go further, for example, and accurately price more complicated securities

insights.claim: Grantham's book notes the average historical U.S. stock return is a function of average historical valuation, citing an average trailing P/E of 14 (on reported, not forward operating, earnings); Hussman notes that even assuming today's record profit margins are permanent, the S&P 500's current trailing P/E stands at 30, roughly double that historical average.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: A trailing P/E more than double the long-run historical average implies investors should expect materially below-average long-term returns from current levels even under generous margin assumptions.
insights.quote: the trailing P/E of the S&P 500 currently stands at 30
insights.locator: an average trailing P/E of 14

insights.claim: Grantham's own GMO research found no stable relationship between economic growth and stock returns, because valuation multiples already price in expected growth and payout rates, so paying up for anticipated rapid growth does not deliver a free lunch.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Investors chasing high-growth names/sectors on the assumption that growth alone drives superior returns are ignoring that the growth is typically already reflected in the price.
insights.quote: there is no stable relationship between economic growth and stock returns.
insights.locator: Among countless chestnuts

insights.claim: Grantham argues that if an asset's price rose simply because it was in favor with investors (rather than for fundamental reasons), the buyer is not being rewarded for taking risk but punished for it.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: A direct warning against momentum-chasing crowded, sentiment-driven trades — popularity itself is framed as a risk factor, not a return driver.
insights.quote: If the assets you bought got pushed up in price simply because they were in favor with investors, then you are not going to be rewarded for taking a risk; you are going to be punished for it.
insights.locator: Yet another

insights.claim: Grantham's view that the stock market cares about the present, not the future ('things get worse when they're perfect and get better when they're ugly'), was the basis for his constructive pivot in his March 2009 piece 'Reinvesting when Terrified,' made at the bottom of the global financial crisis.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: The same present-conditions-over-narrative framework that called the 2009 bottom is the logic Grantham and Hussman now apply to argue current valuations are extreme, not because of a forecast but because of present conditions.
insights.quote: Things will get worse when they’re perfect and get better when they’re ugly.
insights.locator: The stock market, by and large, doesn’t give a rat’s tail

insights.claim: Grantham predicts that current long-run prospects for the broad market are as poor as almost any other time in history, implying investors face either a dismal return forever or a hefty bear market followed by a return to normal.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Frames the bear case as a binary outcome set (permanent valuation re-rating downward vs. a sharp correction) rather than a gradual mean-reversion, useful for scenario planning.
insights.quote: Simple arithmetic suggests you’ll either have a dismal return forever or a hefty bear market followed by a normal return.
insights.locator: On the current situation in the financial markets

insights.claim: Grantham recounts that after GMO clients fired the firm for sticking to its discipline through an unpopular period, not one of those clients returned once the firm was vindicated, and the firm's subsequent rapid asset growth came from being willing to lose more business than competitors rather than chasing short-term popularity.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Illustrates the real career/business cost of maintaining a contrarian discipline through drawdowns in relative performance — a cautionary data point for any manager or advisor considering capitulating to prevailing sentiment.
insights.quote: We were simply willing to lose more business than the other guys.
insights.locator: Our business exploded

themes.theme: The Hedging Enhancement: Finding “Roses in the Garbage”

insights.claim: Hussman has further extended his September 2024 hedging implementation so that 'trap door' conditions (extreme valuations combined with ragged/divergent market internals) — previously only eligible for reduced hedge intensity — can now be reclassified as fully 'constructive with a safety net,' removing the upside cap on returns while keeping downside protection.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: This is the article's central actionable disclosure: Hussman's funds can now participate more fully in bubble-stage rallies even while maintaining a bearish macro view, changing the expected return profile of the funds going forward regardless of whether the bubble bursts.
insights.locator: How I learned to love the bubble (even before it bursts)

insights.claim: Periods reclassified as 'roses' (constructive-with-safety-net carved out of otherwise bearish 'trap door' conditions) make up just over 2% of total market history, but about 15% of weekly periods over the most recent 1-, 3-, and 5-year horizons, and account for more than half of the S&P 500's total return over those horizons.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: A small subset of time periods drives the majority of recent market gains, meaning missing or capturing this narrow window matters disproportionately more than broad market exposure.
insights.quote: they easily account for more than half of the S&P 500 total return over these horizons.
insights.locator: Taken together, 2% of market history may not seem like much

insights.claim: In 2025, most of the S&P 500's overall gain was concentrated in just 7 weeks, which fall within the roughly 2% of market history reclassified as 'roses.'
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Illustrates extreme concentration of 2025 returns in a handful of weeks, reinforcing that broad-based, diversified time-in-market exposure was far less important than being positioned for specific short bursts.
insights.quote: In 2025, most of the overall gain in the S&P 500 was concentrated in just 7 weeks
insights.locator: The green periods often persist for only a week or two

insights.claim: Selloffs that occur amid very high bullish sentiment and low bearish sentiment reflect 'stubborn optimism' rather than panic, and are followed by 'fast, furious, prone-to-failure' rallies with striking regularity even near bubble peaks — though the S&P 500 still often sees a 15-25% drawdown in the months that follow, so these rallies are not safe to trade without a hedge.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Gives a specific, falsifiable pattern (lopsided bullish sentiment plus a selloff) that Hussman uses to time short-term constructive positioning, alongside an explicit caveat that a 15-25% drawdown can still follow.
insights.quote: followed by “fast, furious, prone-to-failure” rallies with striking regularity, even though they often occur near bubble peaks
insights.locator: As an example – in certain conditions

insights.claim: Hussman states his discipline doesn't depend on the bubble ever bursting: he expects it to end in tears, but says 'if it doesn't: We. Don't. Care.' because nothing in the investment discipline forces reliance on any particular scenario.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: A direct statement that the fund's return profile is now designed to be indifferent to whether the bubble persists or collapses, a materially different risk stance than a typical 'permabear' short-volatility posture.
insights.quote: I absolutely expect this bubble to end in tears. But if it doesn’t: We. Don’t. Care.
insights.locator: The Bubble Term, August 14, 2025

insights.claim: As currently adapted, Hussman's investment discipline classifies about 64% of historical periods as constructive or strongly bullish overall, including 40% of periods over just the past decade.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Counters the perception that Hussman's discipline is reflexively bearish; the framework is bullish a majority of the time historically, with bearishness concentrated in specific valuation/internals regimes.
insights.locator: Keep in mind that our most important adaptations

insights.claim: Strongly bullish (ideally leveraged) classifications applied to nearly half of all periods from 1940-2010, but due to increasingly extreme valuations, the discipline classified only about 25% of periods between 2010-2020 as strongly bullish, and none at all since 2020.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Shows a structural decline in fully-leveraged-bullish opportunities over time as valuations have risen, explaining why the fund has appeared persistently defensive in the post-2020 period.
insights.locator: As our investment discipline stands today

insights.claim: Since 2020, about 25% of periods are classified 'constructive (with a safety net)' rather than strongly bullish, and this subset alone accounts for the entire gain of the S&P 500 since 2020.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: A striking concentration statistic: three-quarters of the time since 2020 has contributed zero net return in Hussman's framework, with all gains concentrated in the hedged-constructive quarter of periods.
insights.quote: this subset of periods accounts for the entire gain of the S&P 500 since 2020.
insights.locator: Due to increasingly extreme valuations

insights.claim: Roughly 19% of historical periods are classified 'hard negative' (bearish), and Hussman says this is the only portion of his 'permabear' label that remains accurate and can reasonably be expected to continue.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Hussman explicitly narrows his own bearish reputation to a specific ~19% subset of conditions, implying the remaining ~81% of the time his discipline is not simply 'bearish,' contrary to his public image.
insights.quote: That 19% block of conditions is the only portion of my well-earned “permabear” label that remains accurate
insights.locator: Just over 19% of historical market conditions

insights.claim: Drawing on his late teacher Thich Nhat Hanh's 'roses and garbage' nonduality teaching, Hussman argues that market labels like 'bullish' and 'bearish' are only tools for choosing action, not literal descriptions of reality, and clinging to them can blind an investor to opportunity within a nominally 'bearish' regime.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: The philosophical framing directly motivated a concrete portfolio-construction change (the hedging enhancement), showing how Hussman's non-financial worldview fed into a specific quantitative model revision.
insights.locator: Roses and garbage

insights.claim: Hussman's summary framing is that 'a bubble is a crash on its way to becoming, a crash is a bull market on its way to becoming' — meaning loving a bubble doesn't mean betting on its permanence, just retaining enough flexibility, ideally with a safety net, to participate in whatever regime currently prevails.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Reframes bubble/crash not as opposing bets but as sequential states of the same object, arguing for a hedged-participation posture over binary bull/bear positioning.
insights.quote: A bubble is a crash on its way to becoming. A crash is a bull market on its way to becoming.
insights.locator: A bubble is a crash on its way to becoming

insights.claim: Hussman signals he will likely never issue a literal 'bullish' call as a top-signal for readers waiting to sell at the bubble's peak, suggesting the article's own title ('How I Learned To Love the Bubble') is as close to that signal as readers will ever get.
insights.speaker: @@HussmanFunds
insights.so_what: Warns readers not to wait for an explicit bullish pronouncement from Hussman as a market-top indicator; his framework is designed to never produce one even at literal valuation extremes.
insights.quote: the title of this comment may be as much as you can ever hope to get
insights.locator: For anyone waiting for me to turn bullish
